mappings.rationale: Directly relevant to the cultural and literary developments across Latin American nations in the period since 1920.
Directly covers Brazil since 1930 including the Vargas dictatorship, military rule, return to civilian government, and economic development discussed in subsection G.
Directly relevant to the post-revolutionary Mexican political system, the PRI's dominance, and the reforms of Obregon, Calles, and Cardenas discussed in subsection A.
Relevant to the Cuban Revolution, the Bolivian National Revolution (1952), and the numerous revolutionary movements and military coups discussed throughout the section.
Relevant to the persistent struggle between democracy and dictatorship across Latin America, from military juntas to democratic transitions discussed throughout.
Relevant to the numerous authoritarian regimes including Trujillo in the Dominican Republic, the Somoza family in Nicaragua, Pinochet in Chile, and military governments across the region.
Relevant to Castro's Cuban Socialism, alignment with the Soviet bloc, and the ideological dimension of Cold War conflicts in Latin America discussed in subsections B.2.c and throughout.
Relevant to socialist governments and movements including Allende's Marxist administration in Chile, the Sandinistas in Nicaragua, and socialist parties across the region.
Relevant to populist leaders and movements including Peron in Argentina and Chavez in Venezuela discussed in subsections C.1 and F.1.
Relevant to the numerous civil wars discussed including El Salvador, Guatemala, Colombia, Nicaragua, and the guerrilla insurgencies throughout Central America.
Relevant to the nationalist economic policies, expropriation of foreign industries, and nation-building efforts discussed across multiple Latin American countries.
Relevant to the economic development models, foreign investment, nationalization policies, and market-oriented reforms discussed throughout the section.
Relevant to the economic reforms and austerity measures implemented across Latin America in the 1980s and 1990s, including Chile's post-Pinochet economic policies.
Relevant to NAFTA, foreign trade relations, and the integration of Latin American economies into the global market discussed in subsections A.4.b and throughout.
Relevant to human rights abuses under military dictatorships, the Duvalier regime's political terror, scorched-earth campaigns in Guatemala, and Pinochet's repression.
Relevant to guerrilla insurgencies, drug-related terrorism in Colombia, and political violence discussed in subsections B.1 and C.2.
Relevant to U.S.-Latin American diplomatic relations, the Good Neighbor Policy, the Cuban missile crisis, and the Panama Canal treaties.
Relevant to the Cold War dimension of Latin American politics including the Cuban Revolution, Bay of Pigs, Cuban missile crisis, and U.S. sponsorship of Contra insurgency.
Relevant to U.S. military interventions, CIA-sponsored coups, economic embargoes, and diplomatic relations with Latin American nations discussed throughout.
Relevant to the emigration of Cubans to the U.S., Central American refugee flows, and tensions over illegal immigration discussed in subsections A.4.b and B.2.c.iv.
Relevant to the influx of Central American refugees, Cuban emigration waves, and displacement from civil wars discussed throughout.
Relevant to drug trafficking and associated corruption in Colombia and its effects on politics and society discussed in subsection C.2.c.
Relevant to the continued U.S. imperial influence, military interventions, and economic domination in Latin America and the Caribbean.
Relevant to the independence of new Caribbean nations and the end of European colonial possessions in the region discussed in subsection B.2.d.
Relevant to the economic development patterns, commodity dependence, foreign debt crises, and structural adjustments across Latin American economies.
Relevant to the widespread rural poverty, economic difficulties, and social inequality that characterized many Latin American nations discussed throughout.

mappings.vsiTitle: The Great Depression and The New Deal
mappings.rationale: Relevant to the Great Depression's impact on Latin American economies including Bolivia's mining industry, Chile's economic crises, and Cuba discussed across multiple subsections.
